Referential Integrity Management on Microsoft SQL Server

The MSS connector for Microsoft SQL Server (MS SQL) provides complete DBMS support for referential integrity. Relationships between foreign keys and primary keys or candidate keys are supported.

Referential integrity is handled mainly by MS SQL. Referential integrity is also checked between entities that are not included in a form.

MS SQL uses triggers to maintain referential integrity. However, you must create these triggers before you can use them. The triggers are created in SQL scripts using the Create Script utility.

Note: You must execute the command file that is created using the Create Script utility, before using your application in a production environment.

The CAS, RES, and NUL specifications in the relationship definition are translated into triggers for cascading delete, validation, and update. Although MS-SQL enforces referential integrity constraints, Uniface still checks in forms for the restricted delete constraint (RES) between a one entity and the many entity. This is standard Uniface behavior, but is not sufficient to make table creation on the fly acceptable in a production environment.

Limitation

Microsoft SQL Server does not allow the creation of DBMS referential integrity controls on views.

Triggers

Triggers in Microsoft SQL Server are SQL statements that are executed in an event-driven way. You usually have no knowledge of these triggers or their effect. The table shows which triggers you can create for each table:

Table 1. Triggers in MS-SQL
Trigger When triggered
Insert When a user inserts a new record in the table
Update When a user updates a record in the table
Delete When a user deletes a record in the table

For example, the Delete trigger of the EMPLOYEE table could ensure that the corresponding records in the SALARY table are deleted if the user deletes a record in the EMPLOYEE table.

The generated Delete trigger handles referential integrity. It does this by doing the following:

  • Checking if corresponding records in the many table still exist.
  • Generating an error if the relationship uses the RES (restricted delete) definition.
  • Deleting any corresponding records if the relationship uses the CAS (cascading delete) definition.
  • Nullifying the appropriate records if the relationship uses the NUL (nullify if deleted) definition.

In the case of Insert control, you are not allowed to insert a foreign key unless the foreign key is NULL, or there is a matching key in the one table.

Update control has the following restrictions:

  • You are not allowed to change the value of a key on the one table if the original value is linked to a foreign key.
  • You are not allowed to update a foreign key unless the new value is NULL, or has a matching key in the one table.

Nested Triggers

Microsoft SQL Server allows the use of nested triggers. This means that if a user deletes a record, the Delete trigger is activated for that table. If the table has a one-to-many relationship with another table and the referential integrity definition is CAS, records are deleted in the many table. This in turn activates the Delete trigger of the many table.

Transactions

Trigger coding forms a part of the transaction. If an error is returned when a trigger is activated, this causes the whole transaction to be rolled back. This is not a cause for concern with the ProcScript statements and triggers that Uniface generates by default. However, you need to be aware of this if you change the defaults.

Generated Triggers and Characteristics

The table shows the types of referential integrity that you can create using the MSS connector:

Table 2. Types of referential integrity that can be created with the MSS connector
Action Type of referential integrity
Insert Restrictive (RES)
Update Restrictive (RES)
Delete Restrictive (RES), cascading (CAS), and nullify (NUL)

Trigger Creation

The following triggers are created:

  • Insert—one Insert trigger is created for each many table. The Insert trigger controls the restrictive inserts for all relationships.
  • Update—one Update trigger is created for each table. The Update trigger controls the restrictive updates for all relationships.
  • Delete—one Delete trigger is created for each one table. The Delete trigger controls all restrictive, cascading, and nullify deletes.

Trigger names are created by appending a suffix to the table name. If a table name is longer than 21 characters, only the first 21 characters are used to generate the trigger name. The 21st position truncation point is used on all platforms. For example, the trigger names for a table called cust are as follows:

cust_TR_I /* Insert trigger */
cust_TR_U /* Update trigger */
cust_TR_D /* Delete trigger */

Referential Integrity Violations

When a restrictive relationship is violated, the transaction which caused the violation is rolled back.

Data Conversion

The procedure for data conversion into tables that participate in a relationship differs to data conversion into tables that are not in a relationship.

To insert data into a table which participates in a relationship, load the data in an order which preserves referential integrity at all times. Alternatively, temporarily drop the constraints enforcing referential integrity, load the data into the tables, and re-apply the constraints.
